coredata.dc:title: Enhanced methanol oxidation using porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite synthesized with controlled morphology
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Van-Toan
Kim, Hyunchul
Lee, Doyeon
coredata.dc:description: Palladium (Pd) has received significant attention for its role as a catalyst in many electrochemical reactions that occur in energy-conversion devices. In this study, a porous Pd/carbon dot (porous Pd/Cdot) nanocomposite was synthesized via a simple method; the materials were characterized using various physical techniques. The electrocatalytic behavior of the prepared products toward methanol oxidation was investigated. Electrochemical tests demonstrated that the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite showed excellent electrocatalytic activity and stability when electrooxidizing methanol in alkaline media, suggesting that this nanostructure had promising applications in fuel cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methanol oxidation reaction
Porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite
Controlled morphology
Direct methanol fuel cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite and non-porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and (b) high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HR-SEM) images of porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite. (c) SEM and (d) HR-SEM image of non-porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite.

(a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and (b) and (c) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticle. (d) TEM and (e) and (f) HR-TEM images of non-porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticle. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS)–elemental mapping images of (c1) porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticle and (f1) non-porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticle with (c2) and (f2) overlapped image, (c3) and (f3) Pd atoms, and (c4) and (f4) C atoms.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of porous (red line) and non-porous (black line) Pd/Cdot nanoparticles. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Pd 3d and (c) C 1 s elements of porous and non-porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticles.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry (CV) results of various catalysts in an N2-saturated KOH solution (0.1 M) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1.(b) Stripping curves for CO oxidation at different potentials of KOH (0.1 M) solution at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1.

(a) CV results of various catalysts toward MOR in 0.1 M of KOH + 1.0 M of CH3OH under saturated N2 conditions at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. (b) Chronoamperometric curves at constant potential to produce a current density of 167 A/g for the Pt/C, Pd/C, and porous Pd/Cdot catalysts in the 0.1 M of KOH + 1.0 M of CH3OH electrolyte at 25 °C.
Crystallinity of non-porous and porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticles.

row.entry:
Crystall
ine area for each peak

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Crystallite sizes of non-porous and porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticles.
Electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) value obtained from cyclic voltammetry (CV) and CO stripping methods.

head.ce:title: Enhanced methanol oxidation using porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite synthesized with controlled morphology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanbat National University, 125 Dongseo-daero, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanbat National University, 125 Dongseo-daero, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Future Automotive Engineering, Kongju National University, 1223-24(7-15 205), Cheonandaero, Seobuk-Gu, Cheonan-Si, Chungnam 31080, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Future Automotive Engineering, Kongju National University, 1223-24(7-15 205), Cheonandaero, Seobuk-Gu, Cheonan-Si, Chungnam 31080, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Palladium (Pd) has received significant attention for its role as a catalyst in many electrochemical reactions that occur in energy-conversion devices. In this study, a porous Pd/carbon dot (porous Pd/Cdot) nanocomposite was synthesized via a simple method; the materials were characterized using various physical techniques. The electrocatalytic behavior of the prepared products toward methanol oxidation was investigated. Electrochemical tests demonstrated that the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite showed excellent electrocatalytic activity and stability when electrooxidizing methanol in alkaline media, suggesting that this nanostructure had promising applications in fuel cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) have attracted considerable attention owing to their high energy conversion efficiency, low operating temperature, and portability [1,2]. The methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) that occurs in DMFCs is complicated by the presence of COad intermediate species that can poison the active sites, hampering the further conversion of CO to CO2 [2,3]. Platinum (Pt)-based catalysts are the most active monometallic catalysts used for MORs; however, they have certain major disadvantages that have hindered their large-scale industrial applications [4,5]. Regarding these drawbacks, the increasingly high price and poor stability of Pt have contributed to the extremely-high fabrication cost and reduced average life expectancy of DMFCs [6–8]. Consequently, a practical solution is required to reduce the cost of fuel-cell catalysts and enhance their lifespan. Owing to all this, Pd metal is being highlighted as an excellent alternative to Pt, which is scarcely available. Particularly, Pd and Pt share similar physical and chemical properties [9], with Pd occurring significantly more abundantly on the Earth and displaying an excellent electrocatalytic activity [10–13]. Pd metal has a strong corrosion resistance to electrolytes with high oxidation potential and strong alkalinity, providing it with extreme chemical stability when exposed to harsh reaction conditions [14,15].

Generally, the size and morphology (solid or porous) of precious-metal nanostructures strongly affect their electroactivity and durability. Regarding their various morphologies, because they have greater surface areas, higher porosities, and corner/edge atoms with a richer concentration of available active sites, porous nanostructures with highly-open structures generally exhibit improved electroactivity compared to that shown by conventional solid nanostructures [16–29]. Additionally, porous structures exhibit excellent electrochemical durability because of their interconnected architecture, which can effectively suppress two coarsening mechanisms, namely Ostwald ripening and particle coalescence; thus, these structures possess outstanding self-stability even under harsh electrochemical conditions [17–25].

Herein, we report a facile and scalable synthetic procedure for obtaining porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposites with controlled morphologies. These porous Pd/Cdot catalysts exhibited higher electrochemical oxidation activities than commercial Pd/C catalysts did. Particularly, the porous Pd/Cdot catalyst exhibited the highest activity for the electrochemical oxidation of methanol, with values close to those of state-of-the-art Pt/C (20 wt%) catalysts. We characterized the porous Pd/Cdot catalysts using chronoamperometry, further verifying that they showed better steady-state electrolysis activities for methanol oxidation in alkaline media than pure Pd/C and Pt/C nanoparticles did, suggesting that porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposites have considerable potential as replacements for Pt-electrocatalysts in DMFCs.

Potassium tetrachloropalladate (K2PdCl4) (99.99 % trace metal basis); ascorbic acid (AA) Nafion solution (5 wt%); and commercial platinum on carbon (Pt/C) catalysts (20 wt%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. A commercial palladium on carbon (Pd/C) catalyst (20 wt%) was provided by Premetek. Carbon Vulcan XC 72 was acquired from Fuel Cell Store Co.; isopropyl alcohol (IPA, HPLC grade) and nitric acid (HNO3, 60 %) were purchased from Duksan Chemical Co.; and all the other chemical reagents were used directly without further purification. Deionized (DI) water (18 MΩ) was used in all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite and non-porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (Cdots) have been prepared previously [30]. For synthesizing the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite, 104.16 µmol (34 mg) of K2PdCl4 and 2.42 mmol (29 mg) of Cdot were dissolved in 50 mL of DI water. An aqueous solution (20 mL) containing 100 mg of AA was quickly added; and the resultant solution was stirred for 2–5 min and placed in an oven at 30 °C for 5 min until the solution changed in color to brownish-gray. An identical synthetic procedure was used to prepare the non-porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite; formic acid was used as the reducing agent instead of AA.

Pipettes were used to remove all the samples from the vials. Then, the samples were centrifuged, washed with DI water, and repeatedly rinsed with ethanol to remove any excess reducing reagents (AA and formic acid).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to observe the detailed morphologies of the samples. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were conducted with Cu K-α radiation at 1600 W, and the crystallite sizes were calculated using the Scherrer equation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a spectrometer equipped with a hemispherical electron analyzer with Mg K-α (1253.6 eV) at 100 W with passing energies of 50 and 20 eV, respectively, in the survey analysis and high-resolution regions. The C 1 s line (284.6 eV) was used to correct the charges of all the XPS spectra. The XPSPEAK41 software was employed to perform peak fitting and quantification; a Shirley-type background was employed for all the peaks.

The porous and non-porous Pd/Cdot samples were tested as electrocatalysts for an MOR. Their performances were compared with those of commercial Pt/C (20 wt%) and Pd/C (20 wt%). Rotating disk electrode (RDE) studies were carried out at 25 °C in a conventional three-compartment electrochemical glass cell. A glassy carbon RDE (diameter of 0.071 cm2) was used as the catalyst substrate. This electrode was polished with 0.05 μm alumina before each test to obtain a mirror finish, and it was rinsed with triply-distilled water in an ultrasonic bath. An Ag/AgCl electrode and Pt wire were used as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively.

All the catalysts were deposited onto the working electrode using ink for further examining their electrochemical behavior. Typically, 4 mg of the electrocatalyst, 30 μL of a 5 wt% Nafion solution (Aldrich), and 970 μL of Milli-Q water were dispersed in an ultrasonic bath for 45 min to obtain a homogenous ink. Then, 3 μL of this ink was poured onto the electrode and dried at room temperature for 15 min, resulting in a homogenous coating. The catalyst loading was approximately 2.46 μg for the commercial Pt/C and Pd/C catalysts and porous and non-porous Pd/Cdot and Pd/Cdot electrodes.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) experiments were conducted to clean and activate the electrode surface. Prior to each CV measurement, the electrolyte was purged with nitrogen gas for 30 min. The non-porous Pd/Cdot, porous Pd/Cdot, Pt/C, and Pd/C samples were cycled at 100 mV/s between − 0.15 and 1.1 V until reproducible voltammograms were obtained. The performances of these samples toward methanol oxidation were investigated using potentiodynamic CV techniques. The electrolyte was then saturated with nitrogen for 30 min. The CV curves were obtained in potential ranges between − 0.8 and 0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl at room temperature.

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of each catalyst was determined through analysis using CO stripping voltammetry (oxidation of pre-adsorbed CO). Typically, CO was bubbled during stirring while the working electrode was set at a constant potential of 20 mV for 30 min. The electrolyte was then purged with nitrogen for 40 min to remove the CO. Next, three consecutive potential scans between − 1.0 and 0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl at 10 mV/s were recorded.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was employed to examine the morphology of the prepared products, as shown in Fig. 1 . The as-prepared products with AA as a reducing agent have sphere-like structures with rough surfaces and a diameter ranging between 63.3 and 118.3 nm (Fig. 1(a) and (b)). When AA was used as the reducing agent, a raspberry-like structure was formed, as shown in the high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (HR-SEM) image in Fig. 1(b); the products with this structure were the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposites. In contrast, the as-prepared products with formic acid as the reducing agent showed an agglomerated structure with smooth surfaces in the size range between 60.5 and 126.2 nm (Fig. 1(c) and (d)). When AA was used a reducing agent, a dense structural shape was observed, as shown in the HR-SEM image in Fig. 1(d); the products with this shape were the non-porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposites.

TEM was used to observe the morphology of the as-prepared products; the results are shown in Fig. 2 . A raspberry-like nanostructure can observe for every obtained particle of the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite (Fig. S1, Fig. 2(a)), while the non-porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposites exhibit a dense structural shape (Fig. S2, Fig. 2(d)); this indicated that the type of reducing agent significantly affected the resulting morphologies and structural shapes of the nanocomposites. The magnified HR-TEM image of the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite (Fig. 2(c)) from the red region in Fig. 2(b) shows an interplanar spacing of 0.224 nm, which corresponds to the (111) lattice spacing of the face-centered cubic (FCC) Pd, whereas the Pd/Cdot nanocomposite shows a slightly higher interplanar spacing of 0.225 nm (Fig. 2(e) and (f)). To verify whether composites of Cdots and Pd nanoparticles were present in both the as-synthesized products, EDS mapping (Fig. 2(c1)–(c4) and Fig. 2(f1)–(f4)) was performed. The resulting patterns displayed a uniform distribution of Pd and carbon atoms throughout the non-porous and porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposites, providing strong evidence for the formation of nanocomposites between the Pd atoms and Cdots.

Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of the porous and non-porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticles. For both the samples, the peaks at around 40, 46, 68, 82 and 87° represent the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) reflection planes, respectively. The XRD patterns of both types of nanoparticles were consistent with those in the Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS) (No. 98–004-1517), confirming the formation of Pd nanoparticles with a cubic (FCC) crystal structure. An essential crystallite parameter for determining the electrocatalytic activity of catalyst materials is the variation in the proportion of parameters related to the crystallite surface. The crystallinity (X) and crystallite size (D) of the porous and non-porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticles were calculated using Eq. (1) and the Scherrer equation [22]. The crystallinity and crystallite size are summarized in Table 1 and Table 2 .
(1)
X
( % )
=
Area
o f
c r y s t a l l i n e
p e a k s
Area
o f
a l l
p e a k s
( c r y s t a l l i n e + a m o r p h o u s )
× 100
(2)
D =
K λ
β c o s ( θ )
where D is the crystalline size (nm); K is the Scherrer constant (=0.9); λ is the wavelength of the X-ray source (0.15406 nm); β (rad) is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the corresponding peaks; and θ is the peak position (rad).

XPS was performed to analyze the elemental composition of the porous and non-porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticles. The XPS survey scan showed the presence of Pd and C in both types of nanoparticle samples (Fig. 4 (a)), confirming that the Pd nanoparticles had formed composites with the carbon dots. The peak intensities of carbon in the porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticles were much stronger than those in the non-porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticles, confirming that Cdots could more easily form composites with a porous structure than with a dense structure in Pd nanoparticles. The Pd 3d spectra of the porous and non-porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticles (Fig. 4(b)) were split into four peaks. The binding energy states of the Pd 3d spectra were approximately 335.51 and 340.84 eV, corresponding to Pd in the zero-valent states of Pd0 3d5/2 and Pd0 3d3/2, respectively. Two weaker doublets at 336.56 and 341.85 eV can be assigned to oxidized Pd in the divalent states of either PdO or Pd(OH)2 [33,34]. Pd atoms on the surface can easily oxidize to PdO, contributing to the presence of Pd2+ [34,35]. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the C 1 s peaks at 284.77, 286.58 and 288.87 eV were assigned to the existence of four carbon components, namely CC, CO (expoxy/hydroxyls), CO (carbonyl/ketone), and O-CO carboxyl [34,36].

Fig. 5(a) shows the CV curve of the as-prepared non-porous Pd/Cdot and porous Pd/Cdot catalysts in the range of − 0.9 to 0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl in 0.1 M of KOH at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. There is a pair of peaks corresponding to the hydrogen adsorption/absorption region between the potential range of − 0.78 and − 0.25 V (Eq. (3)).
(3) Pd + H2O + e- ↔ Pd-Hads + OH–

The oxidation of Pd atoms to form PdOH/PdO species (Eqs. (4) and (5)) appeared with a continuously increasing potential in the forward scan.
(4) Pd + OH– → Pd-OHads + e-
(5) Pd-OHads + OH– → PdO + H2O + e-

The oxidized PdO species were reduced in the backward scan at ca. − 0.25 V (Eq. (6)) [37].
(6) PdO + H2O + 2e- → Pd + 2OH–

The ECSA of all the Pd-containing catalysts was derived from the Coulombic charge (Q) associated with the OH adsorption–desorption on the Pd site (Eqs. (4)–(6)) [35]. Therefore, the ECSA of both the non-porous and porous Pd/Cdot catalysts could be determined using the reduction region of the PdO peak, as shown in Eq. (7) and listed in Table 3 .
(7)
ECSA
=
Q 0
Q r
×
m
ct
where Q0 (mC) is the charge associated with the reduction of the PdO species, mcat is the loading amount of Pd, and Qr (assuming 0.405 mC/cm2) is the charge required for reducing the PdO monolayer on the Pd surface.

In case of CO stripping voltammetry, active Pd sites will adsorb CO gas (eq (8)) and oxygen-containing species (OH–) (eq (9)) through a Langmuir-Hinshelwood (LH) mechanism in alkaline solution [38–40]. The.
(8) Pd + CO + * → PdCOad
(9) Pd + OH– + → PdOHad + e-

The adsorbed CO (PdCOad) on porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposites is then oxidized by a nearest-neighbor oxygen-containing species (PdOHad) to release CO2 and active Pd sites as following equation (eq (10)) [38–40].
(10) PdCOad + PdOHad → CO2 + 2Pd + H+ + e-

CO stripping voltammetry has been widely used to characterize the resistance of CO to catalyst poisoning and measure the ECSA of electrocatalysts [30,41,42]. In the first half of the cycle, the hydrogen desorption region was significantly suppressed owing to the saturation coverage of CO on the catalyst surface (Fig. 4(b)). The broad current peaks, corresponding to CO oxidation, were generally found between − 0.2 and 0.2 V for the Pd-containing catalysts whereas the CO oxidation peaks of the Pt/C catalyst were between − 0.4 and − 0.1 V. The peak position of the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite was negatively shifted, compared to the peak positions of the Pd/C and Pd/Cdot catalysts. Owing to the lower oxidation potential of CO, the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite showed a weaker binding with CO and stronger tolerance to CO than the Pd/C and Pd/Cdot catalysts did [43]. The enhanced CO tolerance of the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite could be attributed to its unique structure and large crystallite size [32]. Fig. 5 (b) shows the CO stripping peaks of the samples. The amount of adsorbed CO was evaluated by integrating the CO stripping peak that was corrected for double-layer capacitance. The ECSA of both the non-porous and porous Pd/Cdot catalysts was determined using Eq. (11) and is listed in Table 3 [37].
(11)
ECSA =
Q
CO
Q r
x
m
ct
where QCO (mC) is the charge associated for an assumed monolayer of linearly adsorbed CO; mcat is the loading amount of Pd; and Qr (assuming 420 μC/cm2) is the charge required for oxidizing the adsorbed CO.

A higher ECSA can accelerate the electrocatalytic activity and thereby increase the overall MOR performance [44–46]. As shown in Table 3, the porous Pd/Cdot nanoparticles exhibited a much larger ECSA than the Pd/C and non-porous Pd/Cdot catalysts did, which can be attributed to their higher crystallinity, higher crystallite size, and unique structure. Interestingly, under the CV method, the calculated ECSA value of the Pd/C catalyst was almost equal to that of the non-porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite, but under the CO stripping method, the calculated value of the Pd/C catalyst was much lower than that of the Pd/Cdot nanocomposite. This result could be attributed to the synergistic interaction between Pd and Cdot, which may facilitate greater CO removal on the surface of the Pd/Cdot nanocomposites than on the surface of Pd/C, resulting in the acceleration of the MOR performance of the porous and non-porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposites.

The MOR was studied under potentiodynamic CV in a 0.1 M KOH + 1.0 M CH3OH solution using commercial Pt/C as a reference. The CV curves in Fig. 6 (a) have two oxidation peaks, namely the ones at 38.9 mV and – 0.17 V, which correspond to the oxidation of methanol in the anodic sweep and the removal of carbonaceous species that are not completely oxidized in the anodic sweep, respectively. Regarding the peaks of the catalysts, all shifted more greatly in the positive and negative scan directions than the peak of the Pt/C catalyst did. Regarding the catalysts, the non-porous Pd/Cdot catalyst exhibited the highest overall current density toward the MOR; this current density was much higher than that of commercial Pd/C but still slightly lower than that of the Pt/C catalyst. Although the onset potential of the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite was lower than that of Pd/C catalyst and the non-porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite, it remained higher than that of the Pt/C catalyst. These results implied that the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite facilitated the oxidation of methanol better than the Pd/C catalyst did and less effectively than the Pt/C catalyst did. The slightly negative shift in the onset potential of the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite compared to that of the Pd/C catalyst may be attributed to the change in electronic interactions between the Pd and Cdot.

Chronoamperometric experiments were carried out to evaluate the long-term stability of the Pt/C, Pd/C, non-porous Pd/Cdot, and porous Pd/Cdot catalysts for methanol oxidation. Fig. 6(b) shows the chronoamperometric curves of the different catalysts at a constant potential with a current density of 167 A/g in the 0.1 M of KOH + 1.0 M of CH3OH solution. All the catalysts showed a rapid decrease in current density between 0 and 1500 s, resulting from the accumulation of adsorbed reaction intermediates on their surfaces. After 1500 s, the current density gradually decreased to 5000 s. Compared with the other catalysts, the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite maintained the highest current density throughout the process, demonstrating that it had a better durability for MORs.

In this study, Pd/Cdot nanocomposites were successfully synthesized using two reducing agents that controlled their morphology (either porous or non-porous). The as-prepared porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite was applied as an electrocatalyst toward the oxidation of methanol in alkaline media. Regarding the electrochemical results, the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite exhibited a greater electrocatalytic performance for the oxidation of methanol than the Pd/C and non-porous Pd/Cdot catalysts did. Additionally, the porous Pd/Cdot nanocomposite exhibited a better stability and electrochemical performance than the commercial Pt/C and Pd/C catalysts did, partially due to its stable crystalline structure as well as the synergistic interaction between the Pd and Cdots.

This research was partly funded by the Basic Science Research Program and Regional Innovation Strategy (RIS) through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), Ministry of Education, and Ministry of Science, ICT, and Future Planning (NRF-2022R1C1C1011570, 2021R1I1A3048752, 2021RIS-004), and also supported by Korea Basic Science Institute (National research Facilities and Equipment Center) grant funded by the Ministry of Education.( 2020R1A6C101A187).
Data will be available upon request.

Van-Toan Nguyen: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Hyunchul Kim: Conceptualization, Data curation, Visualization. Doyeon Lee: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.110548.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Electroanal. Chem. 921 (2022) 116705.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient synthesis of PdIr nanocatalysts with controllable surface composition for electrochemical oxidation of methanol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Fu, P. Wang, R.H. Zhang, L.Y. Yan, Z. Cheng, X. Zhang, X.W. Zhou, Efficient synthesis of PdIr nanocatalysts with controllable surface composition for electrochemical oxidation of methanol, Fuel. 332 (2023) 1–10.
